hükûmeti]][[uk:Виконавча влада Японії]][[ur:حکومت جاپان]][[vi:Chính phủ Nhật Bản]][[zh:日本国政府]] {{short description|Constitutional monarchy which governs Japan}} {{good article}} {{Infobox political system | name = Government of Japan | native_name = {{nobold|日本国政府}} | image = [[file:Flag of Japan.svg|240px]] | caption = [[Flag of Japan]] | type = {{ill|Unitary state|en|Unitary Councillors|qid=Q548911|lt=House of Councillors|1=House of Councillors}} | upperhouse_speaker = {{ill|Masakazu Sekiguchi|en|Masakazu Sekiguchi|qid=Q6782441|lt=Masakazu Sekiguchi|1=Masakazu Sekiguchi}} | upperhouse_speaker_title = {{ill|President of the House of Councillors|en|President of the House of Councillors|qid=Q28869611|lt=President of the House of Councillors|1=President of the House of Councillors}} | upperhouse_appointer = | lowerhouse = {{ill|House of Representatives (Japan)|en|House of Representatives (Japan)|qid=Q659587|lt=House of Representatives|1=House of Representatives (Japan)}} | lowerhouse_speaker = {{ill|Fukushiro Nukaga|en|Fukushiro Nukaga|qid=Q1207174|lt=Fukushiro Nukaga|1=Fukushiro Nukaga}} | lowerhouse_speaker_title = {{ill|Speaker of the House of of Japan}} | current_hog = {{ill|Shigeru Ishiba|en|Shigeru Ishiba|qid=Q1335527|lt=Shigeru Ishiba|1=Shigeru Ishiba}} | appointer_hog = Emperor | title_hosag = | current_hosag = | appointer_hosag = | cabinet = {{ill|Cabinet of Japan|en|Cabinet of Japan|qid=Q529012|lt=Cabinet of Japan|1=Cabinet of Japan}} | current_cabinet = {{ill|Second Ishiba Cabinet|en|Second Ishiba Cabinet|qid=Q131143210|lt=Second Ishiba Cabinet|1=Second Ishiba cabinet}} | cabinet_leader = Prime Minister | cabinet_appointer = Prime Minister | cabinet_designator = Emperor | cabinet_hq = [[Naikaku Sōri Daijin Kantei]]
(Prime Minister's Office of Japan) | cabinet_ministries = | judiciary_head = [[Yukihiko Imasaki]] | judiciary_head_title = [[Chief Justice of the Supreme Court of Japan|Chief Justice of the Supreme Court]] | courts = | court = {{ill|Supreme Court of Japan|en|Supreme Court of Japan|qid=Q1142255|lt=Supreme Court of Japan|1=Supreme Court of Japan}} | chief_judge = | court_seat = [[Chiyoda, Tokyo|Chiyoda]], {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} | court1 = | chief_judge1 = | court_seat1 = | formation = {{start date and age|1867}} }} {{Politics of Japan}} {{Infobox Chinese | kanji = {{linktext|日|本|国|政|府}} | hiragana = にほんこくせいふ (formal)
にっぽんこくせいふ (informal) | romaji = Nihonkoku Seifu (formal)
Nipponkoku Seifu (informal) }} The '''Government of Japan''' is the {{ill|Central government|en|Central government|qid=Q1320217|lt=Central government|1=Central government}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It consists of [[:en:legislative|legislative]], {{ill|Executive (government)|en|Executive (government)|qid=Q35798|lt=executive|1=Executive (government)}} and {{ill|Judiciary|en|Judiciary|qid=Q105985|lt=Judiciary|1=Judiciary}} branches and functions under the framework established by the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|qid=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan|1=Constitution of Japan}}. Japan is a {{ill|Unitary state|en|Unitary state|qid=Q179164|lt=Unitary state|1=Unitary state}}, containing forty-seven {{ill|Administrative divisions of Japan|en|Administrative divisions of Japan|qid=Q1850442|lt=administrative divisions|1=Administrative divisions of Japan}}, with the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}} as its {{ill|Head of state|en|Head of state|qid=Q48352|lt=Head of state|1=Head of state}}.{{cite web|title=The World Factbook Japan|url=https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/countries/japan/|publisher=[[Central Intelligence Agency]]|access-date=5 September 2015}} His role is ceremonial and he has no powers related to the Government.{{cite constitution|article=4(1)|section=1|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} Instead, it is the {{ill|Cabinet of Japan|en|Cabinet of Japan|qid=Q529012|lt=Cabinet|1=Cabinet of Japan}}, comprising the {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=prime minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}} and the {{ill|Minister (government)|en|Minister (government)|qid=Q83307|lt=ministers of state|1=Minister (government)}}, that directs and controls the government and the {{ill|Civil service of Japan|en|Civil service of Japan|qid=Q5124690|lt=civil service|1=Civil service of Japan}}. The Cabinet has the [[:en:executive power|executive power]] and is formed by the prime minister, who is the {{ill|Head of government|en|Head of government|qid=Q2285706|lt=Head of government|1=Head of government}}.{{cite constitution|article=65|section=5|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}}{{cite constitution|article=68(1)|section=5|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} The Prime Minister is nominated by the {{ill|National Diet|en|National Diet|qid=Q202384|lt=National Diet|1=National Diet}} and {{ill|Imperial Investiture|en|Imperial Investiture|qid=Q2279140|lt=appointed|1=Imperial Investiture}} to office by the Emperor.{{cite constitution|article=67(1)|section=5|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}}{{cite constitution|article=6(1)|section=1|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} The current cabinet is the {{ill|Second Ishiba Cabinet|en|Second Ishiba Cabinet|qid=Q131143210|lt=Second Ishiba Cabinet|1=Second Ishiba cabinet}}, which was formed on 11 November 2024 and is led by prime minister {{ill|Shigeru Ishiba|en|Shigeru Ishiba|qid=Q1335527|lt=Shigeru Ishiba|1=Shigeru Ishiba}} who assumed office on 1 October 2024. The country has had a {{ill|Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)|en|Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)|qid=Q232595|lt=Liberal Democratic|1=Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)}}–{{ill|Komeito|en|Komeito|qid=Q1061354|lt=Komeito|1=Komeito}} {{ill|Coalition government|en|Coalition government|qid=Q837416|lt=coalition|1=Coalition government}} {{ill|minority government|en|minority government|qid=Q114793|lt=minority government|1=Minority government}} since {{ill|2024 Japanese general election|en|2024 Japanese general election|qid=Q109383045|lt=2024|1=2024 Japanese general election}}. The National Diet is the {{ill|Legislature|en|Legislature|qid=Q11204|lt=Legislature|1=Legislature}}, the organ of the Legislative branch. The Diet is {{ill|Bicameralism|en|Bicameralism|qid=Q189445|lt=bicameral|1=Bicameralism}}, consisting of two houses with the [[:en:House of Councilors|House of Councilors]] being the {{ill|upper house|en|upper house|qid=Q637846|lt=upper house|1=Upper house}}, and the {{ill|House of Representatives (Japan)|en|House of Representatives (Japan)|qid=Q659587|lt=House of Representatives|1=House of Representatives (Japan)}} being the {{ill|lower house|en|lower house|qid=Q375928|lt=lower house|1=Lower house}}. The members of both houses of the Diet are directly elected by the {{ill|people|en|people|qid=Q2472587|lt=people|1=People}}, who are the source of {{ill|Sovereignty|en|Sovereignty|qid=Q42008|lt=Sovereignty|1=Sovereignty}}.{{cite constitution|article=1|section=1|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} The Diet is defined as the supreme organ of sovereignty in the Constitution. The {{ill|Supreme Court of Japan|en|Supreme Court of Japan|qid=Q1142255|lt=Supreme Court|1=Supreme Court of Japan}} and other lower courts make up the Judicial branch and have all the judicial powers in the state. The Supreme Court has ultimate judicial authority to interpret the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|qid=Q237082|lt=constitution|1=Constitution of Japan}} and the power of {{ill|Judicial review|en|Judicial review|qid=Q898871|lt=Judicial review|1=Judicial review}}. The judicial branch is {{ill|Judicial independence|en|Judicial independence|qid=Q3059233|lt=independent|1=Judicial independence}} from the executive and the legislative branches.{{cite constitution|article=76(2)|section=6|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} {{ill|Judge|en|Judge|qid=Q16533|lt=Judge|1=Judge}}s are nominated or appointed by the Cabinet and never removed by the executive or the legislature except during {{ill|impeachment|en|impeachment|qid=Q480498|lt=impeachment|1=Impeachment}}. The Government of Japan is based in the capital of {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, where the {{ill|National Diet Building|en|National Diet Building|qid=Q1338856|lt=National Diet Building|1=National Diet Building}}, the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], the Supreme Court, the [[Naikaku Sōri Daijin Kantei|Prime Minister's Office]] and the ministries are all located. == History == Before the [[Meiji Restoration]], Japan was ruled by the government of successive military {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}. During this period, effective power of the government resided in the Shōgun, who officially ruled the country in the name of the Emperor.{{cite book|last1=Chaurasla|first1=Radhey Shyam|title=History of money|date=2003|publisher=Atlantic Publishers and Distributors|location=New Delhi|isbn=9788126902286|page=10}} The Shōgun were the hereditary military governors, with their modern rank equivalent to a {{ill|generalissimo|en|generalissimo|qid=Q191079|lt=generalissimo|1=Generalissimo}}. Although the Emperor was the sovereign who appointed the Shōgun, his roles were ceremonial and he took no part in governing the country.{{cite journal|last1=Koichi|first1=Mori|title=The Emperor of Japan: A Historical Study in Religious Symbolism|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|date=December 1979|volume=6/4|pages=535–540}} This is often compared to the present role of the Emperor, whose official role is to appoint the Prime Minister.{{cite journal|last1=Bob Tadashi|first1=Wakabayashi|title=In Name Only: Imperial Sovereignty in Early Modern Japan|journal=Journal of Japanese Studies|date=1991|volume=7|issue=1|pages=25–57}} The return of political power to the Emperor (to the Imperial Court) at the end of 1867 meant the resignation of Shōgun [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]], agreeing to "be the instrument for carrying out" the Emperor's orders.{{Cite web |title=19世紀後半、黒船、地震、台風、疫病などの災禍をくぐり抜け、明治維新に向かう（福和伸夫） - エキスパート |url=https://news.yahoo.co.jp/expert/articles/4d57ba83d5e41aac42e5017f84dc3147e53dc0ff |access-date=2025-01-31 |website=Yahoo!ニュース |language=ja}}{{cite book|last1=Satow|first1=Ernest Mason|title=A Diplomat in Japan|date=Aug 23, 2013|publisher=Project Gutenberg|page=282|url=https://www.gutenberg.org/files/43541/43541-h/43541-h.htm|access-date=5 September 2015}} This event restored the country to Imperial rule and the proclamation of the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan|1=Empire of Japan}}. In 1889, the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|qid=Q122804|lt=Meiji Constitution|1=Meiji Constitution}} was adopted in a move to strengthen Japan to the level of western nations, resulting in the first {{ill|Parliamentary system|en|Parliamentary system|qid=Q166747|lt=Parliamentary system|1=Parliamentary system}} in Asia.{{cite news|title=Asia's First Parliament|url=https://timesmachine.nytimes.com/timesmachine/1891/01/26/103292581.pdf|newspaper={{ill|The New York Times|en|The New York Times|qid=Q9684|lt=The New York Times|1=The New York Times}}|access-date=5 September 2015}} It provided a form of mixed {{ill|Constitutional monarchy|en|Constitutional monarchy|qid=Q41614|lt=constitutional|1=Constitutional monarchy}}-{{ill|Absolute monarchy|en|Absolute monarchy|qid=Q184558|lt=Absolute monarchy|1=Absolute monarchy}} (a {{ill|semi-constitutional monarchy|en|semi-constitutional monarchy|qid=Q43675451|lt=semi-constitutional monarchy|1=Semi-constitutional monarchy}}), with an independent judiciary, based on the {{ill|Prussia|en|Prussia|qid=Q38872|lt=Prussia|1=Prussia}}n model of the time.{{cite web|title=The Nature of Sovereignty in Japan, 1870s–1920s|url=http://www.colorado.edu/cas/tea/becoming-modern/3-nature/nature-of-sovereignty-lesson.pdf|publisher=University of Colorado Boulder|access-date=5 September 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150923222611/http://www.colorado.edu/cas/tea/becoming-modern/3-nature/nature-of-sovereignty-lesson.pdf|archive-date=23 September 2015|url-status=dead}} A new aristocracy known as the ''[[kazoku]]'' was established. It merged the ancient court nobility of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, the ''[[kuge]]'', and the former ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'', feudal lords subordinate to the ''shōgun''.{{cite book|last1=Lebra|first1=Takie Sugiyama|title=Japanese social organization|date=1992|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824814205|page=51|edition=1}} It also established the [[:en:Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Diet]], consisting of the {{ill|House of Representatives (Japan)|en|House of Representatives (Japan)|qid=Q659587|lt=House of Representatives|1=House of Representatives (Japan)}} and the {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|en|House of Peers (Japan)|qid=Q715139|lt=House of Peers|1=House of Peers (Japan)}}. Members of the House of Peers were made up of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial Family|1=Imperial House of Japan}}, the Kazoku, and those nominated by the Emperor,{{cite constitution|article=34|section=3|country=the Empire of Japan|language=ja|ratified=1889|url=http://www.ndl.go.jp/constitution/e/etc/c02.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} while members of the House of Representatives were elected by direct male suffrage.{{cite constitution|article=35|section=3|country=the Empire of Japan|language=ja|ratified=1889|url=http://www.ndl.go.jp/constitution/e/etc/c02.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} Despite clear distinctions between powers of the executive branch and the Emperor in the Meiji Constitution, ambiguity and contradictions in the Constitution eventually led to a [[:en:Taisho Political Crisis|political crisis]].{{cite book|last1=Skya|first1=Walter A.|title=Japan's holy war the ideology of radical Shintō ultranationalism|date=2009|publisher=Duke University Press|location=Durham|isbn=9780822392460|page=40}} It also devalued the notion of [[:en:Civilian control of the military|civilian control over the military]], which meant that the military could develop and exercise a great influence on politics.{{cite book|last1=Martin|first1=Bernd|title=Japan and Germany in the modern world|date=2006|publisher=Berghahn Books|location=New York [u.a.]|isbn=9781845450472|page=31|edition=1. paperback}} Following the end of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, the present {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|qid=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan|1=Constitution of Japan}} was adopted. It replaced the previous Imperial rule with a form of Western-style {{ill|Liberal democracy|en|Liberal democracy|qid=Q192125|lt=Liberal democracy|1=Liberal democracy}}.{{cite web|title=The Constitution: Context and History|url=http://www.hartpub.co.uk/pdf/samples/9781841137926sample.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150924025048/http://www.hartpub.co.uk/pdf/samples/9781841137926sample.pdf |archive-date=24 September 2015 |publisher=Hart Publishing |access-date=5 September 2015}} As of 2020, the Japan Research Institute found the national government is mostly analog, because only 7.5% (4,000 of the 55,000) administrative procedures can be completed entirely online. The rate is 7.8% at the Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry, 8% at the Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications, and only 1.3% at the Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries.{{cite web|title=Japan's analog government struggles to accept anything online |url=https://asia.nikkei.com/Politics/Japan-s-analog-government-struggles-to-accept-anything-online |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200618145803/https://asia.nikkei.com/Politics/Japan-s-analog-government-struggles-to-accept-anything-online |archive-date=18 June 2020 |publisher=Nikkei |access-date=18 June 2020}} On 12 February 2021, {{ill|Tetsushi Sakamoto|en|Tetsushi Sakamoto|qid=Q7706925|lt=Tetsushi Sakamoto|1=Tetsushi Sakamoto}} was appointed as the Minister of Loneliness to alleviate social isolation and loneliness across different age groups and genders.{{cite web |title=Japan appoints 'minister' to help people home alone |website=Nikkei |date=February 13, 2021 |url=https://asia.nikkei.com/Spotlight/Coronavirus/Japan-appoints-minister-of-loneliness-to-help-people-home-alone |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210223060539/https://asia.nikkei.com/Spotlight/Coronavirus/Japan-appoints-minister-of-loneliness-to-help-people-home-alone |archive-date=February 23, 2021}} == The Emperor == [[File:20101129gikai1.jpg|thumb|left|[[:en:Emperor Akihito|Emperor Akihito]] and {{ill|Empress Michiko|en|Empress Michiko|qid=Q87610|lt=Empress Michiko|1=Empress Michiko}} seated in the Chamber of the House of Councillors of the National Diet, with members of the imperial family, the cabinet, and prime minister {{ill|Naoto Kan|en|Naoto Kan|qid=Q47846|lt=Naoto Kan|1=Naoto Kan}} giving the government's speech in front of the assembled members of parliament (2010)]] The emperor of Japan is the head of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial Family|1=Imperial House of Japan}} and the ceremonial {{ill|Head of state|en|Head of state|qid=Q48352|lt=Head of state|1=Head of state}}. He is defined by the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|qid=Q237082|lt=Constitution|1=Constitution of Japan}} to be "the symbol of the State and of the unity of the people". However, his role is entirely ceremonial and representative in nature. As explicitly stated in [[s:Constitution of Japan#CHAPTER I. THE EMPEROR|article 4]] of the Constitution, he has no powers related to government.{{cite constitution|article=4|section=1|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} [[s:Constitution of Japan#CHAPTER I. THE EMPEROR|Article 6]] of the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|qid=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan|1=Constitution of Japan}} delegates the Emperor the following ceremonial roles: # Appointment of the prime minister as designated by the Diet. # Appointment of the chief justice of the Supreme Court as designated by the Cabinet. While the {{ill|Cabinet of Japan|en|Cabinet of Japan|qid=Q529012|lt=Cabinet|1=Cabinet of Japan}} is the source of executive power and most of its power is exercised directly by the {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=prime minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}}, several of its powers are exercised through the Emperor. The powers exercised via the Emperor, as stipulated by [[s:Constitution of Japan#CHAPTER I. THE EMPEROR|Article 7]] of the Constitution, are: # Promulgation of amendments of the constitution, laws, cabinet orders and treaties. # Dissolution of the House of Representatives. # Proclamation of general election of members of the Diet. # Attestation of the appointment and dismissal of Ministers of State and other officials as provided for by law, and of full powers and credentials of Ambassadors and Ministers. # Attestation of general and special amnesty, commutation of punishment, reprieve, and restoration of rights. # Awarding of honors. # Attestation of instruments of ratification and other diplomatic documents as provided for by law. # Receiving foreign ambassadors and ministers. # Performance of ceremonial functions. These powers are exercised in accordance with the binding advice of the Cabinet. The emperor is known to hold the nominal ceremonial authority. For example, he is the only person that has the authority to appoint the {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=prime minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}}, even though the {{ill|National Diet|en|National Diet|qid=Q202384|lt=Diet|1=National Diet}} has the power to designate the person fitted for the position. One such example can be prominently seen in the 2009 Dissolution of the {{ill|House of Representatives (Japan)|en|House of Representatives (Japan)|qid=Q659587|lt=House of Representatives|1=House of Representatives (Japan)}}. The House was expected to be dissolved on the advice of the prime minister, but was temporarily unable to do so for the next general election, as both the Emperor and Empress were visiting {{ill|Canada|en|Canada|qid=Q16|lt=Canada|1=Canada}}.{{cite web|url=http://ronbun.apa.co.jp/images/pdf/2009jyusyou_saiyuusyu_english.pdf|title=Did the Emperor of Japan really fall from being a ruler to a symbol|publisher=Tsuneyasu Takeda. Instructor, Keio University.|access-date=28 August 2015|archive-date=4 February 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190204080055/http://ronbun.apa.co.jp/images/pdf/2009jyusyou_saiyuusyu_english.pdf|url-status=dead}}{{cite web|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iB4I1SWf8jc| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140607084911/http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iB4I1SWf8jc| archive-date=2014-06-07 | url-status=dead|title=2009 Japanese Emperor and Empress Visited in Vancouver|website=[[YouTube]]|access-date=18 June 2015}} In this manner, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}}'s modern role is often compared to those of the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}} period and much of {{ill|History of Japan|en|History of Japan|qid=Q130436|lt=Japan's history|1=History of Japan}}, whereby the emperor held great symbolic {{ill|Authority|en|Authority|qid=Q174834|lt=Authority|1=Authority}} but had little {{ill|political power|en|political power|qid=Q2101636|lt=political power|1=Political power}}; which is often held by others nominally appointed by the emperor himself. Today, a legacy has somewhat continued for a retired prime minister who still wields considerable power, to be called a ''Shadow Shogun''.{{cite news|title=A shadow of a shogun|date=2012|url=http://www.economist.com/node/21557788|access-date=18 June 2015|newspaper=The Economist}} Unlike his {{ill|Monarchies in Europe|en|Monarchies in Europe|qid=Q3494968|lt=European counterparts|1=Monarchies in Europe}}, the emperor is not the source of sovereign power and the government does not act under his name. Instead, the emperor represents the state and appoints other high officials in the name of the state, in which the {{ill|Japanese people|en|Japanese people|qid=Q161652|lt=Japanese people|1=Japanese people}} hold sovereignty.{{cite web|title=Fundamental Structure of the Government of Japan|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/fundamental_e.html|publisher=Prime Minister's Official Residence Website|access-date=1 September 2015}} [[s:Constitution of Japan#CHAPTER I. THE EMPEROR|Article 5]] of the Constitution, in accordance with the {{ill|Imperial Household Law|en|Imperial Household Law|qid=Q1324733|lt=Imperial Household Law|1=Imperial Household Law}}, allows a [[Sesshō and Kampaku|regency]] to be established in the emperor's name, should the emperor be unable to perform his duties.{{cite constitution|article=5|section=1|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} On November 20, 1989, the {{ill|Supreme Court of Japan|en|Supreme Court of Japan|qid=Q1142255|lt=Supreme Court|1=Supreme Court of Japan}} ruled it {{ill|Sovereign immunity|en|Sovereign immunity|qid=Q2457196|lt=doesn't have judicial power over the emperor|1=Sovereign immunity}}.{{cite web|title= 最高裁判所判例集 事件番号 平成1(行ツ)126|website=Courts in Japan|url= https://www.courts.go.jp/app/hanrei_jp/detail2?id=52451 |access-date=August 10, 2020}} The {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan|1=Imperial House of Japan}} is said to be the oldest continuing hereditary monarchy in the world.{{cite news|title=Japan's royal family pose for unusual New Year photo|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/worldnews/asia/japan/10545850/Japans-royal-family-pose-for-unusual-New-Year-photo.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220112/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/worldnews/asia/japan/10545850/Japans-royal-family-pose-for-unusual-New-Year-photo.html |archive-date=2022-01-12 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live|date=2014|access-date=18 June 2015|newspaper=The Daily Telegraph}}{{cbignore}} According to the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], Japan was founded by the Imperial House in 660 BC by Emperor Jimmu.{{cite book|last1=Kitagawa|first1=Joseph M.|title=On understanding Japanese religion|date=1987|publisher=Princeton University Press|location=Princeton, N.J.|isbn=9780691102290|page=[https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita/page/145 145]|url=https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita/page/145}} Emperor Jimmu was the first Emperor of Japan and the ancestor of all of the Emperors that followed.{{cite book|last1=Smith|first1=Robert J.|title=Ancestor worship in contemporary Japan|date=1974|publisher=Stanford University Press|location=Stanford, Calif.|isbn=9780804708739|pages=[https://archive.org/details/ancestorworshipi0000smit/page/8 8–9]|edition=[Repr.].|url-access=registration|url=https://archive.org/details/ancestorworshipi0000smit/page/8}} He is, according to [[Japanese mythology]], the direct descendant of Amaterasu, the sun goddess of the native [[Shinto]] religion, through [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]], his great-grandfather.{{cite web|title=Kojiki|url=http://www.ceres.dti.ne.jp/~alex-x/wakan/kojikix1.html|publisher=[[Ō no Yasumaro]]|access-date=5 September 2015|archive-date=30 April 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200430112156/http://www.ceres.dti.ne.jp/~alex-x/wakan/kojikix1.html|url-status=dead}}{{cite web|title=Nihon Shoki|url=http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_01940/ri05_01940_0003/ri05_01940_0003.pdf|publisher=[[Prince Toneri]]|access-date=5 September 2015}} The current emperor of Japan is {{ill|Naruhito|en|Naruhito|qid=Q217096|lt=Naruhito|1=Naruhito}}. He was officially [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthroned]] on May 1, 2019, following the abdication of his father.{{cite web|title=Enthronement and Ceremonies|url=http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/seido/sokui.html|publisher={{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}|access-date=1 September 2015}}{{cite web|title=The 20th Anniversary of His Majesty the Emperor's Accession to the Throne|url=http://www.mofa.go.jp/j_info/japan/imperial/20anniversary/activity.html|publisher={{ill|Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|en|Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|qid=Q222241|lt=Ministry of Foreign Affairs|1=Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)}}|access-date=1 September 2015}} He is styled as His Imperial Majesty, and his reign bears the era name of Reiwa. [[:en:Fumihito, Prince Akishino|Fumihito]] is the heir presumptive to the {{ill|Chrysanthemum Throne|en|Chrysanthemum Throne|qid=Q1981690|lt=Chrysanthemum Throne|1=Chrysanthemum Throne}}. == Executive == The executive branch of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} is headed by the {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=prime minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}}. The prime minister is the head of the {{ill|Cabinet of Japan|en|Cabinet of Japan|qid=Q529012|lt=Cabinet|1=Cabinet of Japan}}, and is designated by the legislative organ, the {{ill|National Diet|en|National Diet|qid=Q202384|lt=National Diet|1=National Diet}}. The Cabinet consists of the [[:en:Ministers of State|Ministers of State]] and may be appointed or dismissed by the Prime Minister at any time. Explicitly defined to be the source of executive {{ill|power (social and political)|en|power (social and political)|qid=Q25107|lt=power|1=Power (social and political)}}, it is in practice, however, mainly exercised by the prime minister. The practice of its powers is responsible to the Diet, and as a whole, should the Cabinet lose {{ill|Confidence and supply|en|Confidence and supply|qid=Q21406588|lt=confidence|1=Confidence and supply}} and support to be in office by the Diet, the Diet may dismiss the Cabinet ''en masse'' with a {{ill|motion of no confidence|en|motion of no confidence|qid=Q329547|lt=motion of no confidence|1=Motion of no confidence}}.{{cite constitution|article=69|section=5|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} === Prime minister === [[File:Go-shichi no kiri crest 2.svg|thumb|right|258x258px|Crest of the Prime Minister of Japan]] The {{Nihongo|{{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=Prime Minister of Japan|1=Prime Minister of Japan}}|内閣総理大臣}} is designated by the {{ill|National Diet|en|National Diet|qid=Q202384|lt=National Diet|1=National Diet}} and serves a term of four years or less; with no limits imposed on the number of terms the Prime Minister may hold. The Prime Minister heads the Cabinet and exercises "control and supervision" of the executive branch, and is the {{ill|Head of government|en|Head of government|qid=Q2285706|lt=Head of government|1=Head of government}} and {{ill|Commander-in-chief|en|Commander-in-chief|qid=Q380782|lt=Commander-in-chief|1=Commander-in-chief}} of the {{ill|Japan Self-Defense Forces|en|Japan Self-Defense Forces|qid=Q275488|lt=Japan Self-Defense Forces|1=Japan Self-Defense Forces}}.{{cite constitution|article=72|section=5|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} The prime minister is vested with the power to present {{ill|Bill (law)|en|Bill (law)|qid=Q686822|lt=bills|1=Bill (law)}} to the Diet, to sign laws, to declare a {{ill|state of emergency|en|state of emergency|qid=Q216227|lt=state of emergency|1=State of emergency}}, and may also dissolve the Diet's House of Representatives at will.{{cite constitution|article=7|section=1|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} The prime minister presides over the Cabinet and appoints, or dismisses, the other Cabinet [[:en:Ministers of State|ministers]]. Both houses of the National Diet designates the Prime Minister with a ballot cast under the {{ill|Two-round system|en|Two-round system|qid=Q615255|lt=run-off|1=Two-round system}} system. Under the Constitution, should both houses not agree on a common candidate, then a joint committee is allowed to be established to agree on the matter; specifically within a period of ten days, exclusive of the period of recess.{{cite constitution|article=67(2)|section=5|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} However, if both houses still do not agree to each other, the decision made by the {{ill|House of Representatives (Japan)|en|House of Representatives (Japan)|qid=Q659587|lt=House of Representatives|1=House of Representatives (Japan)}} is deemed to be that of the National Diet. Upon designation, the Prime Minister is presented with their commission, and then formally {{ill|Imperial Investiture|en|Imperial Investiture|qid=Q2279140|lt=appointed|1=Imperial Investiture}} to office by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}}. As a candidate designated by the Diet, the prime minister is required to report to the Diet whenever demanded.{{cite constitution|article=63|section=4|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} The prime minister must also be both a {{ill|civilian|en|civilian|qid=Q206887|lt=civilian|1=Civilian}} and a member of either house of the Diet.{{cite constitution|article=66(2)|section=5|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} {{Hatnote|For a list of prime ministers prior to 2001, see {{ill|List of prime ministers of Japan|en|List of prime ministers of Japan|qid=Q243185|lt=List of prime ministers of Japan|1=List of prime ministers of Japan}}.}} {| class="wikitable sortable" style="width:100%;" |- ! 103rd: {{ill|Second Ishiba Cabinet|en|Second Ishiba Cabinet|qid=Q131143210|lt=Ishiba II|1=Second Ishiba cabinet}} |} === The Cabinet === [[File:Naikakufu1.jpg|thumb|right|177px|Cabinet Office Building]] [[File:Government Office Complex 2 of Japan 2009.jpg|thumb|right|180px|2nd Building of the Central Government]] The {{Nihongo|{{ill|Cabinet of Japan|en|Cabinet of Japan|qid=Q529012|lt=Cabinet of Japan|1=Cabinet of Japan}}|内閣}} consists of the [[:en:Ministers of State|Ministers of State]] and the Prime Minister. The members of the Cabinet are appointed by the Prime Minister, and under the Cabinet Law, the number of members of the Cabinet appointed, excluding the Prime Minister, must be fourteen or less, but may only be increased to nineteen should a special need arise.{{cite web|title=内閣法|url=http://law.e-gov.go.jp/htmldata/S22/S22HO005.html|publisher=Government of Japan|access-date=1 September 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100425060228/http://law.e-gov.go.jp/htmldata/S22/S22HO005.html|archive-date=25 April 2010|url-status=dead}}{{cite news|title=Toshiaki Endo appointed Olympics minister|url=http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2015/06/25/national/toshiaki-endo-appointed-olympics-minister/|newspaper={{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|qid=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}}|access-date=1 September 2015}} [[s:Constitution of Japan#CHAPTER V. THE CABINET|Article 68]] of the Constitution states that all members of the Cabinet must be {{ill|civilian|en|civilian|qid=Q206887|lt=civilian|1=Civilian}}s and the majority of them must be chosen from among the members of either house of the {{ill|National Diet|en|National Diet|qid=Q202384|lt=National Diet|1=National Diet}}.{{cite constitution|article=68|section=5|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} The precise wording leaves an opportunity for the Prime Minister to appoint some non-elected Diet officials.{{cite constitution|article=68(2)|section=5|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} The Cabinet is required to resign ''en masse'' while still continuing its functions, till the appointment of a new Prime Minister, when the following situation arises: # The Diet's House of Representatives passes a non-confidence resolution, or rejects a confidence resolution, unless the House of Representatives is dissolved within the next ten days. # When there is a vacancy in the post of the Prime Minister, or upon the first convocation of the Diet after a general election of the members of the House of Representatives. Conceptually deriving {{ill|Legitimacy (family law)|en|Legitimacy (family law)|qid=Q378505|lt=legitimacy|1=Legitimacy (family law)}} from the Diet, whom it is responsible to, the Cabinet exercises its power in two different ways. In practice, much of its power is exercised by the Prime Minister, while others are exercised nominally by the Emperor. [[s:Constitution of Japan#CHAPTER V. THE CABINET|Article 73]] of the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|qid=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan|1=Constitution of Japan}} expects the Cabinet to perform the following functions, in addition to general administration: # Administer the law faithfully; conduct affairs of state. # Manage foreign affairs. # Conclude treaties. However, it shall obtain prior or, depending on circumstances, subsequent approval of the Diet. # Administer the {{ill|Civil service of Japan|en|Civil service of Japan|qid=Q5124690|lt=civil service|1=Civil service of Japan}}, in accordance with standards established by law. # Prepare the budget, and present it to the Diet. # Enact cabinet orders in order to execute the provisions of this Constitution and of the law. However, it cannot include penal provisions in such cabinet orders unless authorized by such law. # Decide on general amnesty, special amnesty, commutation of punishment, reprieve, and restoration of rights. Under the Constitution, all laws and cabinet orders must be signed by the competent Minister and countersigned by the Prime Minister, before being formally promulgated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}}. Also, all members of the Cabinet cannot be subject to legal action without the consent of the Prime Minister; however, without impairing the right to take legal action.{{cite constitution|article=75|section5|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} {{Hatnote|For an updated list of members of the Cabinet, see [[Cabinet of Japan#Current cabinet|Current Cabinet of Japan]].}} {{Current cabinet of Japan}} === Ministries and agencies === [[File:Monbusho1.jpg|thumb|right|180px|Agency for Cultural Affairs Office Building]] [[File:Politics Under Constitution of Japan 04.svg|thumb|right|440x369px|Politics of the Constitution of Japan]]{{Main|Ministries of Japan}} The {{Nihongo|{{ill|Ministries of Japan|en|Ministries of Japan|qid=Q15409298|lt=Ministries of Japan|1=Ministries of Japan}}|中央省庁|Chuo shōcho}} consist of eleven executive ministries and the {{ill|Cabinet Office (Japan)|en|Cabinet Office (Japan)|qid=Q6005|lt=Cabinet Office|1=Cabinet Office (Japan)}}. Each ministry is headed by a {{ill|Minister of State|en|Minister of State|qid=Q2632935|lt=Minister of State|1=Minister of State}}, which are mainly senior legislators, and are appointed from among the members of the Cabinet by the Prime Minister. The Cabinet Office, formally headed by the Prime Minister, is an agency that handles the day-to-day affairs of the Cabinet. The ministries are the most influential part of the daily-exercised executive power, and since few ministers serve for more than a year or so necessary to grab hold of the organisation, most of its power lies within the senior {{ill|Bureaucrat|en|Bureaucrat|qid=Q572700|lt=Bureaucrat|1=Bureaucrat}}s.{{cite web|url=http://countrystudies.us/japan/123.htm|publisher=[[Library of Congress Country Studies]]|title=Bureaucrats of Japan|access-date=1 September 2015}} Below is a series of ministry-affiliated government agencies and bureaus responsible for government procedures and activities as of 23 August 2022."Government directory." Tokyo: Cabinet Public Affairs Office, Cabinet Secretariat. *{{ill|Board of Audit|en|Board of Audit|qid=Q615225|lt=Board of Audit|1=Board of Audit}} == Legislative == [[File:Diet of Japan Kokkai 2009.jpg|thumb|279x279px|{{ill|National Diet Building|en|National Diet Building|qid=Q1338856|lt=National Diet Building|1=National Diet Building}}, {{ill|Nagatachō|en|Nagatachō|qid=Q1197269|lt=Nagatachō|1=Nagatachō}}, [[:en:Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo|Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo]]]] {{Main|National Diet}} The legislative branch organ of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} is the {{Nihongo|{{ill|National Diet|en|National Diet|qid=Q202384|lt=National Diet|1=National Diet}}|国会}}. It is a bicameral legislature, composing of a lower house, the {{ill|House of Representatives (Japan)|en|House of Representatives (Japan)|qid=Q659587|lt=House of Representatives|1=House of Representatives (Japan)}}, and an upper house, the [[:en:House of Councillors (Japan)|House of Councillors]]. Empowered by the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|qid=Q237082|lt=Constitution|1=Constitution of Japan}} to be "the highest organ of State power" and the only "sole law-making organ of the State", its houses are both directly elected under a {{ill|Parallel voting|en|Parallel voting|qid=Q1062211|lt=parallel voting system|1=Parallel voting}} and is ensured by the Constitution to have no discrimination on the qualifications of each members; whether be it based on "race, creed, sex, social status, family origin, education, property or income". The National Diet, therefore, reflects the {{ill|Sovereignty|en|Sovereignty|qid=Q42008|lt=Sovereignty|1=Sovereignty}} of the people; a principle of {{ill|Popular sovereignty|en|Popular sovereignty|qid=Q285950|lt=Popular sovereignty|1=Popular sovereignty}} whereby the supreme power lies within, in this case, the {{ill|Japanese people|en|Japanese people|qid=Q161652|lt=Japanese people|1=Japanese people}}.{{cite constitution|article=43(1)|section=4|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} The Diet responsibilities includes the making of laws, the approval of the annual national budget, the approval of the conclusion of treaties and the selection of the {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=prime minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}}. In addition, it has the power to initiate draft constitutional amendments, which, if approved, are to be presented to the people for ratification in a {{ill|referendum|en|referendum|qid=Q43109|lt=referendum|1=Referendum}} before being promulgated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}}, in the name of the {{ill|Japanese people|en|Japanese people|qid=Q161652|lt=people|1=Japanese people}}.{{cite constitution|article=96|section=9|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} The Constitution also enables both houses to conduct investigations in relation to government, demand the presence and testimony of witnesses, and the production of records, as well as allowing either house of the Diet to demand the presence of the Prime Minister or the other Minister of State, in order to give answers or explanations whenever so required. The Diet is also able to {{ill|impeachment|en|impeachment|qid=Q480498|lt=impeach|1=Impeachment}} Court judges convicted of criminal or irregular conduct. The Constitution, however, does not specify the voting methods, the number of members of each house, and all other matters pertaining to the method of election of the each members, and are thus, allowed to be determined for by law.{{cite constitution|article=64(1)|section=4|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} Under the provisions of the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|qid=Q237082|lt=Constitution|1=Constitution of Japan}} and by law, all adults aged over 18 are eligible to vote, with a {{ill|secret ballot|en|secret ballot|qid=Q337987|lt=secret ballot|1=Secret ballot}} and a {{ill|Universal suffrage|en|Universal suffrage|qid=Q319891|lt=Universal suffrage|1=Universal suffrage}}, and those elected have certain protections from apprehension while the Diet is in session.{{cite news|title=Diet enacts law lowering voting age to 18 from 20|url=http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2015/06/17/national/politics-diplomacy/diet-enacts-law-lowering-voting-age-18-20/|access-date=18 June 2015|newspaper={{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|qid=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}}|archive-date=10 October 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010081014/http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2015/06/17/national/politics-diplomacy/diet-enacts-law-lowering-voting-age-18-20|url-status=dead}} Speeches, debates, and votes cast in the Diet also enjoy {{ill|parliamentary privilege|en|parliamentary privilege|qid=Q1616363|lt=parliamentary privilege|1=Parliamentary privilege}}s. Each house is responsible for disciplining its own members, and all deliberations are public unless two-thirds or more of those members present passes a resolution agreeing it otherwise. The Diet also requires the presence of at least one-third of the membership of either house in order to constitute a quorum.{{cite constitution|article=56(1)|section=4|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} All decisions are decided by a majority of those present, unless otherwise stated by the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|qid=Q237082|lt=Constitution|1=Constitution of Japan}}, and in the case of a tie, the presiding officer has the right to decide the issue. A member cannot be expelled, however, unless a majority of two-thirds or more of those members present passes a resolution.{{cite constitution|article=55|section=4|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} Under the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|qid=Q237082|lt=Constitution|1=Constitution of Japan}}, at least one session of the Diet must be convened each year. The {{ill|Cabinet of Japan|en|Cabinet of Japan|qid=Q529012|lt=Cabinet|1=Cabinet of Japan}} can also, at will, convoke extraordinary sessions of the Diet and is required to, when a quarter or more of the total members of either house demands it.{{cite constitution|article=53|section=4|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} During an election, only the House of Representatives is dissolved. The House of Councillors is however, not dissolved but only closed, and may, in times of national emergency, be convoked for an emergency session.{{cite constitution|article=54(2)|section=4|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} The {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}} both convokes the Diet and dissolves the House of Representatives, but only does so on the advice of the {{ill|Cabinet of Japan|en|Cabinet of Japan|qid=Q529012|lt=Cabinet|1=Cabinet of Japan}}. For bills to become law, they are to be first passed by both houses of the {{ill|National Diet|en|National Diet|qid=Q202384|lt=National Diet|1=National Diet}}, signed by the [[:en:Ministers of State|Ministers of State]], countersigned by the {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=prime minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}}, and then finally promulgated by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}}; however, without specifically giving the Emperor the power to oppose legislation. === House of Representatives === [[File:Chamber of the House of Representatives of Japan.jpg|thumb|280x280px|Chamber of the House of Representatives]] The {{Nihongo|{{ill|House of Representatives (Japan)|en|House of Representatives (Japan)|qid=Q659587|lt=House of Representatives of Japan|1=House of Representatives (Japan)}}|衆議院}} is the {{ill|lower house|en|lower house|qid=Q375928|lt=lower house|1=Lower house}}, with the members of the house being elected once every four years, or when dissolved, for a four-year term.{{cite constitution|article=45|section=4|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} As of November 18, 2017, it has 465 members. Of these, 176 members are elected from 11 multi-member constituencies by a party-list system of {{ill|proportional representation|en|proportional representation|qid=Q13365595|lt=proportional representation|1=Proportional representation}}, and 289 are elected from single-member constituencies. 233 seats are required for majority. The House of Representatives is the more powerful house out of the two, it is able to override {{ill|Veto|en|Veto|qid=Q178074|lt=Veto|1=Veto}}es on bills imposed by the [[:en:House of Councillors (Japan)|House of Councillors]] with a two-thirds majority. It can, however, be dissolved by the {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=prime minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}} at will. Members of the house must be of [[:en:Japanese nationality|Japanese nationality]]; those aged 18 years and older may vote, while those aged 25 years and older may run for office in the lower house. The legislative powers of the {{ill|House of Representatives (Japan)|en|House of Representatives (Japan)|qid=Q659587|lt=House of Representatives|1=House of Representatives (Japan)}} is considered to be more powerful than that of the [[:en:House of Councillors (Japan)|House of Councillors]]. While the House of Councillors has the ability to {{ill|Veto|en|Veto|qid=Q178074|lt=Veto|1=Veto}} most decisions made by the House of Representatives, some however, can only be delayed. This includes the legislation of treaties, the budget, and the selection of the {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=prime minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}}. The Prime Minister, and collectively his {{ill|Cabinet of Japan|en|Cabinet of Japan|qid=Q529012|lt=Cabinet|1=Cabinet of Japan}}, can in turn, however, dissolve the House of Representatives whenever intended. While the House of Representatives is considered to be officially dissolved upon the preparation of the document, the House is only formally dissolved by the dissolution ceremony.{{cite web|title=解散と万歳|url=https://www.eda-jp.com/books/giin/20.html|publisher=[[Parti démocrate du Japon]]|access-date=1 September 2015}} The dissolution ceremony of the House is as follows:[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ir57eKebRmc ''Japanese PM dissolves lower house of parliament, calls snap elections 일본 중의원 해''], journal télévisé, [[:en:Arirang News|Arirang News]], 21 novembre 2014 - une partie des phases et éléments la cérémonie peut être vue en arrière-plan # The document is {{ill|Rubber stamp (politics)|en|Rubber stamp (politics)|qid=Q4811251|lt=rubber stamped|1=Rubber stamp (politics)}} by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}}, and wrapped in a purple silk cloth; an indication of a document of state act, done on behalf of the people. # The document is passed on to the {{ill|Chief Cabinet Secretary|en|Chief Cabinet Secretary|qid=Q1078482|lt=Chief Cabinet Secretary|1=Chief Cabinet Secretary}} at the House of Representatives President's reception room. # The document is taken to the Chamber for preparation by the General-Secretary. # The General-Secretary prepares the document for reading by the Speaker. # The Speaker of the {{ill|House of Representatives (Japan)|en|House of Representatives (Japan)|qid=Q659587|lt=House of Representatives|1=House of Representatives (Japan)}} promptly declares the dissolution of the House. # The House of Representatives is formally dissolved. It is customary that, upon the dissolution of the House, members will shout the [[Ten thousand years#Japan|Three Cheers of Banzai]] (萬歲).{{cite news|title=小泉進次郎氏、衆議院解散でも万歳しなかった「なぜ今、解散か」|url=http://www.huffingtonpost.jp/2014/11/22/shinjiro-koizumi_n_6203042.html|work=[[The Huffington Post]]|access-date=1 September 2015}} === House of Councillors === [[File:Japanese diet inside.jpg|thumb|288x288px|Chamber of the House of Councillors]] The {{Nihongo|[[:en:House of Councillors (Japan)|House of Councillors of Japan]]|参議院}} is the {{ill|upper house|en|upper house|qid=Q637846|lt=upper house|1=Upper house}}, with half the members of the house being elected once every three years, for a six-year term. As of November 18, 2017, it has 242 members. Of these, 73 are elected from the 47 prefectural districts, by {{ill|single non-transferable vote|en|single non-transferable vote|qid=Q1307208|lt=single non-transferable vote|1=Single non-transferable vote}}s, and 48 are elected from a nationwide list by {{ill|proportional representation|en|proportional representation|qid=Q13365595|lt=proportional representation|1=Proportional representation}} with [[:en:open lists|open lists]]. The House of Councillors cannot be dissolved by the {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=prime minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}}. Members of the house must be of [[:en:Japanese nationality|Japanese nationality]]; those aged 18 years and older may vote, while those aged 30 years and older may run for office in the upper house. As the House of Councillors can {{ill|Veto|en|Veto|qid=Q178074|lt=Veto|1=Veto}} a decision made by the {{ill|House of Representatives (Japan)|en|House of Representatives (Japan)|qid=Q659587|lt=House of Representatives|1=House of Representatives (Japan)}}, the House of Councillors can cause the House of Representatives to reconsider its decision. The House of Representatives however, can still insist on its decision by overriding the {{ill|Veto|en|Veto|qid=Q178074|lt=Veto|1=Veto}} by the House of Councillors with a two-thirds majority of its members present. Each year, and when required, the {{ill|National Diet|en|National Diet|qid=Q202384|lt=National Diet|1=National Diet}} is convoked at the House of Councillors, on the advice of the Cabinet, for an extra or an ordinary session, by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}}. A short speech is, however, usually first made by the Speaker of the House of Representatives before the Emperor proceeds to convoke the Diet with his {{ill|Speech from the throne|en|Speech from the throne|qid=Q787020|lt=Speech from the throne|1=Speech from the throne}}.{{cite web|title=開会式|url=http://www.sangiin.go.jp/japanese/aramashi/kaikaisiki.html|publisher=House of Councillors|access-date=1 September 2015}} == Judicial == [[File:Supreme Court of Japan 2010.jpg|thumb|287x287px|{{ill|Supreme Court of Japan|en|Supreme Court of Japan|qid=Q1142255|lt=Supreme Court Building|1=Supreme Court of Japan}}, [[:en:Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo|Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo]]]] [[File:Tokyo High Court Building02bs3200.jpg|thumb|285x285px|Tokyo High Court Building]] {{Main|Judicial system of Japan}} The judicial branch of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} consists of the {{ill|Supreme Court of Japan|en|Supreme Court of Japan|qid=Q1142255|lt=Supreme Court|1=Supreme Court of Japan}}, and four other lower courts; the High Courts, District Courts, Family Courts and Summary Courts.{{cite web|url=http://www.courts.go.jp/about/sosiki/gaiyo/index.html|publisher=Supreme Court of Japan |title=Overview of the Judicial System in Japan|access-date=18 June 2015}} Divided into four basic tiers, the Court's independence from the executive and legislative branches are guaranteed by the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|qid=Q237082|lt=Constitution|1=Constitution of Japan}}, and is stated as: "no extraordinary tribunal shall be established, nor shall any organ or agency of the Executive be given final judicial power"; a feature known as the [[:en:Separation of Powers|Separation of Powers]]. [[s:Constitution of Japan#CHAPTER VI. JUDICIARY|Article 76]] of the Constitution states that all the Court judges are independent in the exercise of their own conscience and that they are only bounded by the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|qid=Q237082|lt=Constitution|1=Constitution of Japan}} and the laws.{{cite constitution|article=76|section=6|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} Court judges are removable only by public {{ill|impeachment|en|impeachment|qid=Q480498|lt=impeachment|1=Impeachment}}, and can only be removed, without impeachment, when they are judicially declared mentally or physically incompetent to perform their duties.{{cite constitution|article=78|section=6|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} The Constitution also explicitly denies any power for executive organs or agencies to administer disciplinary actions against judges. However, a {{ill|Supreme Court of Japan|en|Supreme Court of Japan|qid=Q1142255|lt=Supreme Court|1=Supreme Court of Japan}} judge may be dismissed by a majority in a referendum; of which, must occur during the first general election of the {{ill|National Diet|en|National Diet|qid=Q202384|lt=National Diet's|1=National Diet}} {{ill|House of Representatives (Japan)|en|House of Representatives (Japan)|qid=Q659587|lt=House of Representatives|1=House of Representatives (Japan)}} following the judge's appointment, and also the first general election for every ten years lapse thereafter.{{cite constitution|article=79(2)|section=6|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} Trials must be conducted, with judgment declared, publicly, unless the Court "unanimously determines publicity to be dangerous to public order or morals"; with the exception for trials of political offenses, offenses involving the press, and cases wherein the rights of people as guaranteed by the Constitution, which cannot be deemed and conducted privately.{{cite constitution|article=82(2)|section=6|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} Court judges are appointed by the {{ill|Cabinet of Japan|en|Cabinet of Japan|qid=Q529012|lt=Cabinet|1=Cabinet of Japan}}, in attestation of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}}, while the [[:en:Chief Justice of Japan|Chief Justice]] is {{ill|Imperial Investiture|en|Imperial Investiture|qid=Q2279140|lt=appointed|1=Imperial Investiture}} by the Emperor, after being nominated by the {{ill|Cabinet of Japan|en|Cabinet of Japan|qid=Q529012|lt=Cabinet|1=Cabinet of Japan}}; which in practice, known to be under the recommendation of the former Chief Justice.{{cite news|url=http://www.japantimes.co.jp/opinion/2014/03/17/editorials/change-at-the-top-courts-helm/|title=Change at the top court's helm|access-date=18 June 2015|newspaper={{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|qid=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}}}} The {{ill|Law of Japan|en|Law of Japan|qid=Q951841|lt=Legal system|1=Law of Japan}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} has been historically influenced by {{ill|Chinese law|en|Chinese law|qid=Q2135373|lt=Chinese law|1=Chinese law}}; developing independently during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} through texts such as ''{{ill|Kujikata Osadamegaki|en|Kujikata Osadamegaki|qid=Q2705628|lt=Kujikata Osadamegaki|1=Kujikata Osadamegaki}}''.{{cite book|last1=Dean|first1=Meryll|title=Japanese legal system : text, cases & materials|date=2002|publisher=Cavendish|location=London|isbn=9781859416730|pages=55–58|edition=2nd}} It has, however, changed during the [[Meiji Restoration]], and is now largely based on the {{ill|Europe|en|Europe|qid=Q46|lt=Europe|1=Europe}}an {{ill|Civil law (legal system)|en|Civil law (legal system)|qid=Q5950118|lt=civil law|1=Civil law (legal system)}}; notably, the {{ill|civil code|en|civil code|qid=Q1923776|lt=civil code|1=Civil code}} based on the German model still remains in effect.{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://global.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/301097/Japanese-Civil-Code|title=Japanese Civil Code |encyclopedia=Encyclopædia Britannica|access-date=18 June 2015}} A [[:en:Jury in Japan|quasi-jury system]] has recently came into use, and the legal system also includes a bill of rights since May 3, 1947.{{cite web|url=http://core.ecu.edu/hist/tuckerjo/occupation.htm|title=MacArthur and the American Occupation of Japan|access-date=18 June 2015|archive-date=21 March 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170321044255/http://core.ecu.edu/hist/tuckerjo/occupation.htm|url-status=dead}} The collection of {{ill|Six Codes|en|Six Codes|qid=Q2985324|lt=Six Codes|1=Six Codes}} makes up the main body of the {{ill|Law of Japan|en|Law of Japan|qid=Q951841|lt=Japanese statutory law|1=Law of Japan}}. All [[:en:Statutory Law|Statutory Law]]s in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} are required to be {{ill|Rubber stamp (politics)|en|Rubber stamp (politics)|qid=Q4811251|lt=rubber stamped|1=Rubber stamp (politics)}} by the Emperor with the {{Nihongo|{{ill|Privy Seal of Japan|en|Privy Seal of Japan|qid=Q2915131|lt=Privy Seal of Japan|1=Privy Seal of Japan}}|天皇御璽}}, and no Law can take effect without the {{ill|Cabinet of Japan|en|Cabinet of Japan|qid=Q529012|lt=Cabinet's|1=Cabinet of Japan}} signature, the {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=prime minister's|1=Prime Minister of Japan}} countersignature and the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor's|1=Emperor of Japan}} promulgation.{{cite constitution|article=74|section=5|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}}{{cite constitution|article=7(1)|section=1|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}}{{cite web|title=II. The law-making process|url=http://www.clb.go.jp/english/process.html|publisher={{ill|Cabinet Legislation Bureau|en|Cabinet Legislation Bureau|qid=Q1062966|lt=Cabinet Legislation Bureau|1=Cabinet Legislation Bureau}}|access-date=6 September 2015}}{{cite web|title=The Privy Seal and State Seal|url=http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/seido/seido09.html|publisher={{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}|access-date=6 September 2015}}{{cite web|title=Promulgation of Laws|url=http://www.npb.go.jp/en/books/kanpo.html|publisher=[[National Printing Bureau]]|access-date=6 September 2015}} === Supreme Court === [[File:Saikosaibansho.jpg|thumb|Building of the {{ill|Supreme Court of Japan|en|Supreme Court of Japan|qid=Q1142255|lt=Supreme Court of Japan|1=Supreme Court of Japan}}]] The {{Nihongo|{{ill|Supreme Court of Japan|en|Supreme Court of Japan|qid=Q1142255|lt=Supreme Court of Japan|1=Supreme Court of Japan}}|最高裁判所}} is the court of last resort and has the power of {{ill|Judicial review|en|Judicial review|qid=Q898871|lt=Judicial review|1=Judicial review}}; as defined by the Constitution to be "the court of last resort with power to determine the constitutionality of any law, order, regulation or official act".{{cite constitution|article=81|section=6|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}} The Supreme Court is also responsible for nominating judges to lower courts and determining judicial procedures. It also oversees the judicial system, overseeing activities of public prosecutors, and disciplining judges and other judicial personnel.{{cite web|title=Overview of the Judicial System in Japan|url=http://www.courts.go.jp/english/judicial_sys/overview_of/overview/index.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130413023634/http://www.courts.go.jp/english/judicial_sys/overview_of/overview/index.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=April 13, 2013|publisher={{ill|Supreme Court of Japan|en|Supreme Court of Japan|qid=Q1142255|lt=Supreme Court of Japan|1=Supreme Court of Japan}}|access-date=6 September 2015}} === High Courts === The {{Nihongo|[[:en:High Court|High Courts of Japan]]|高等裁判所}} has the jurisdiction to hear appeals to judgments rendered by District Courts and Family Courts, excluding cases under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Supreme Court of Japan|en|Supreme Court of Japan|qid=Q1142255|lt=Supreme Court|1=Supreme Court of Japan}}. Criminal appeals are directly handled by the High Courts, but Civil cases are first handled by District Courts. There are eight High Courts in Japan: the Tokyo, Osaka, Nagoya, Hiroshima, Fukuoka, Sendai, Sapporo, and Takamatsu High Courts. === Penal system === The {{Nihongo|{{ill|penal system of Japan|en|penal system of Japan|qid=Q51756923|lt=penal system of Japan|1=Penal system of Japan}}|矯正施設}} is operated by the {{ill|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|qid=Q1031145|lt=Ministry of Justice|1=Ministry of Justice (Japan)}}. It is part of the {{ill|Criminal justice system of Japan|en|Criminal justice system of Japan|qid=Q5185626|lt=criminal justice system|1=Criminal justice system of Japan}}, and is intended to {{ill|Resocialization|en|Resocialization|qid=Q2638787|lt=resocialize|1=Resocialization}}, {{ill|reform|en|reform|qid=Q900406|lt=reform|1=Reform}}, and {{ill|Rehabilitation (penology)|en|Rehabilitation (penology)|qid=Q2142602|lt=rehabilitate|1=Rehabilitation (penology)}} offenders. The ministry's Correctional Bureau administers the adult prison system, the {{ill|Juvenile delinquency|en|Juvenile delinquency|qid=Q1001125|lt=juvenile correctional system|1=Juvenile delinquency}}, and three of the women's guidance homes,{{cite web|title=Correction Bureau|url=http://www.moj.go.jp/ENGLISH/CB/cb-01.html|publisher={{ill|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|qid=Q1031145|lt=Ministry of Justice|1=Ministry of Justice (Japan)}}|access-date=6 September 2015}} while the Rehabilitation Bureau operates the {{ill|probation|en|probation|qid=Q13961|lt=probation|1=Probation}} and the {{ill|parole|en|parole|qid=Q5357120|lt=parole|1=Parole}} systems.{{cite web|title=Rehabilitation Bureau|url=http://www.moj.go.jp/ENGLISH/RB/rb-01.html|publisher={{ill|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|qid=Q1031145|lt=Ministry of Justice|1=Ministry of Justice (Japan)}}|access-date=6 September 2015}} == Other government agencies == The Cabinet Public Affairs Office's Government Directory also listed a number of government agencies that are more independent from executive ministries."Government Directory". Tokyo: Cabinet Public Affairs Office, Cabinet Secretariat. Retrieved (23 August 2022) from https://www.japan.go.jp/directory/index.html. The list for these types of agencies can be seen below. * {{ill|Japan National Tourism Organization|en|Japan National Tourism Organization|qid=Q11086315|lt=Japan National Tourism Organisation|1=Japan National Tourism Organization}} (JNTO) * {{ill|Japan International Cooperation Agency|en|Japan International Cooperation Agency|qid=Q958590|lt=Japan International Cooperation Agency|1=Japan International Cooperation Agency}} (JICA) * {{ill|Japan External Trade Organization|en|Japan External Trade Organization|qid=Q1364370|lt=Japan External Trade Organisation|1=Japan External Trade Organization}} (JETRO) * {{ill|Japan Foundation|en|Japan Foundation|qid=Q1519715|lt=The Japan Foundation|1=Japan Foundation}} * {{ill|Bank of Japan|en|Bank of Japan|qid=Q333101|lt=Bank of Japan|1=Bank of Japan}} * {{ill|Japan Mint|en|Japan Mint|qid=Q1130777|lt=Japan Mint|1=Japan Mint}} * National Research Bureau of Brewing (NRIB) * {{ill|state guest house|en|state guest house|qid=Q52843464|lt=State House|qid=Q26791586|lt=Kyoto State Guest House|1=Kyoto State Guest House}}) * {{ill|National Archives of Japan|en|National Archives of Japan|qid=Q1072875|lt=National Archives of Japan|1=National Archives of Japan}} * National Women's Education Centre * {{ill|NHK|en|NHK|qid=Q212128|lt=Japan Broadcasting Corporation|1=NHK}} (NHK) * {{ill|National Institutes for Cultural Heritage|en|National Institutes for Cultural Heritage|qid=Q11421271|lt=National Institutes for Cultural Heritage|1=National Institutes for Cultural Heritage}} * Japan Arts Council * {{ill|Japan Society for the Promotion {{ill|JAXA|en|JAXA|qid=Q179103|lt=Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency|1=JAXA}} (JAXA) * {{ill|Japan Science and Technology Agency|en|Japan Science and Technology Agency|qid=Q11595927|lt=Japan Science Organization}} (NEDO) * {{ill|National Institute of Infectious Diseases (Japan)|en|National Institute of Infectious Diseases (Japan)|qid=Q10855561|lt=National Institute of Infectious
