Institute for Labour Policy and Training|1=Japan Institute for Labour Policy and Training}} == Local government == [[File:Japan admin levels.svg|thumb|300px|Administrative divisions of Japan]] {{Administrative divisions of Japan}} According to Article 92 of the Constitution, the {{Nihongo|{{ill|Administrative divisions of Japan|en|Administrative divisions of Japan|qid=Q1850442|lt=local governments of Japan|1=Administrative divisions of Japan}}|地方公共団体}} are local public entities whose body and functions are defined by law in accordance with the principle of local autonomy.{{cite constitution|article=92, 93, 94 and 95|section=8|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}}{{cite web|title=Final Report|url=http://www.shugiin.go.jp/internet/itdb_kenpou.nsf/html/kenpou/report.pdf/$File/report.pdf|website=Research Commission on the Constitution House of Representatives, Japan|access-date=September 3, 2020}} p463-480 The main law that defines them is the [[:en:en:Local Autonomy Law|en:Local Autonomy Law]].{{cite web|title=AUTHORITY OF THE NATIONAL AND LOCAL GOVERNMENTS UNDER THE CONSTITUTION|url=http://scholarship.law.duke.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=4028&context=lcp|publisher=[[Duke University School of Law]]|access-date=6 September 2015}}{{cite web|title=地方自治法について|url= https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_content/000051164.pdf|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=August 22, 2020}}　They are given limited executive and legislative powers by the Constitution. Governors, mayors and members of assemblies are constitutionally elected by the residents. The {{ill|Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|en|Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|qid=Q1322293|lt=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|1=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications}} intervenes significantly in local government, as do other ministries. This is done chiefly financially because many local government jobs need funding initiated by national ministries. This is dubbed as the "thirty-percent autonomy".三割自治 {{cite web|url=http://countrystudies.us/japan/116.htm|title=Local Government|access-date=18 June 2015}} The result of this power is a high level of organizational and policy standardization among the different local jurisdictions allowing them to preserve the uniqueness of their {{ill|Prefecture|en|Prefecture|qid=Q515716|lt=Prefecture|1=Prefecture}}, city, or town. Some of the more collectivist jurisdictions, such as {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} and {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, have experimented with policies in such areas as social welfare that later were adopted by the national government. === Local authorities === Japan is divided into forty-seven {{ill|Administrative divisions of Japan|en|Administrative divisions of Japan|qid=Q1850442|lt=administrative divisions|1=Administrative divisions of Japan}}, the {{ill|Prefectures of Japan|en|Prefectures of Japan|qid=Q50337|lt=prefectures|1=Prefectures of Japan}} are: one metropolitan district ({{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}), two {{ill|Fu (administrative division)|en|Fu (administrative division)|qid=Q839328|lt=urban prefectures|1=Fu (administrative division)}} ({{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} and {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|qid=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}), forty-three rural prefectures, and one "district", [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. Large cities are subdivided into wards, and further split into towns, or precincts, or subprefectures and counties. Cities are self-governing units administered independently of the larger jurisdictions within which they are located. In order to attain city status, a jurisdiction must have at least 500,000 inhabitants, 60 percent of whom are engaged in urban occupations. There are {{ill|Self-governance|en|Self-governance|qid=Q417633|lt=self-governing|1=Self-governance}} towns outside the cities as well as precincts of urban wards. Like the cities, each has its own elected {{ill|Mayor|en|Mayor|qid=Q30185|lt=Mayor|1=Mayor}} and assembly. Villages are the smallest self-governing entities in rural areas. They often consist of a number of rural hamlets containing several thousand people connected to one another through the formally imposed framework of village administration. Villages have mayors and councils elected to four-year terms.{{cite web|title=Local Autonomy Law|url=http://law.e-gov.go.jp/htmldata/S22/S22HO067.html|publisher=Government of Japan|access-date=6 September 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180209183605/http://law.e-gov.go.jp/htmldata/S22/S22HO067.html|archive-date=9 February 2018|url-status=dead}}{{cite web|title=The Large City System of Japan|url=http://www3.grips.ac.jp/~coslog/activity/01/04/file/Bunyabetsu-20_en.pdf|publisher=[[National Graduate Institute for Policy Studies]]|access-date=6 September 2015|archive-date=17 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190917003255/http://www3.grips.ac.jp/~coslog/activity/01/04/file/Bunyabetsu-20_en.pdf|url-status=dead}} === Structure === Each jurisdiction has a chief executive, called a {{Nihongo|governor|知事|chiji}} in prefectures and a {{Nihongo|mayor|市町村長|shichōsonchō}} in municipalities. Most jurisdictions also have a unicameral {{Nihongo|assembly|議会|gikai}}, although towns and villages may opt for direct governance by citizens in a {{Nihongo|general assembly|総会|sōkai}}. Both the executive and assembly are elected by popular vote every four years.{{cite constitution|article=93(2)|section=8|country=Japan|language=en|ratified=1947|url=http://japan.kantei.go.jp/constitution_and_government_of_japan/constitution_e.html|access-date=5 September 2015}}{{cite web|title=Local Autonomy in Japan Current Situation & Future Shape|url=http://www.clair.or.jp/j/forum/other_data/pdf/20100216_soumu_e.pdf|publisher=[[Council of Local Authorities for International Relations]]|access-date=6 September 2015}}{{cite web|title=An Outline of Local Government in Japan|url=http://www.gdrc.org/uem/observatory/logov-japan.pdf|publisher=[[Council of Local Authorities for International Relations]]|access-date=6 September 2015}} Local governments follow a modified version of the [[:en:Separation of Powers|Separation of Powers]] used in the national government. An assembly may pass a [[:en:vote of no confidence|vote of no confidence]] in the executive, in which case the executive must either dissolve the assembly within ten days or automatically lose their office. Following the next election, however, the executive remains in office unless the new assembly again passes a no confidence resolution. The primary methods of local lawmaking are {{Nihongo|[[local ordinance]]|条例|jōrei}} and {{Nihongo|local regulations|規則|kisoku}}. Ordinances, similar to statutes in the national system, are passed by the assembly and may impose limited criminal penalties for violations (up to 2 years in prison and/or 1 million yen in fines). Regulations, similar to cabinet orders in the national system, are passed by the executive unilaterally, are superseded by any conflicting ordinances, and may only impose a fine of up to 50,000 yen. Local governments also generally have multiple {{ill|committee|en|committee|qid=Q865588|lt=committee|1=Committee}}s such as school boards, public safety committees (responsible for overseeing the {{ill|Police|en|Police|qid=Q35535|lt=Police|1=Police}}), personnel committees, election committees and auditing committees.{{cite web|title=The Organization of Local Government Administration in Japan|url=http://www3.grips.ac.jp/~coslog/activity/01/04/file/Bunyabetsu-11_en.pdf|publisher=[[National Graduate Institute for Policy Studies]]|access-date=6 September 2015|archive-date=8 March 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160308003522/http://www3.grips.ac.jp/~coslog/activity/01/04/file/Bunyabetsu-11_en.pdf|url-status=dead}} These may be directly elected or chosen by the assembly, executive or both. Scholars have noted that political contestations at the local level tend not to be marked by strong party affiliation or political ideologies when compared to the national level. Moreover, in many local communities candidates from different parties tend to share similar concerns, e.g., regarding depopulation and how to attract new residents. Analyzing the political discourse among local politicians, Hijino suggests that local politics in depopulated areas is marked by two overarching ideas: "populationism" and "listenism." He writes, "“Populationism” assumes the necessity of maintaining and increasing the number of residents for the future and vitality of the municipality. “Listenism” assumes that no decision can be made unless all parties are consulted adequately, preventing majority decisions taken by elected officials over issues contested by residents. These two ideas, though not fully-fledged ideologies, are assumptions guiding the behavior of political actors in municipalities in Japan when dealing with depopulation."{{Cite journal |last=Hijino |first=Ken Victor Leonard |date=2023-03-27 |title=The politics of depopulation in Japanese municipalities: Ideas and underlying ideologies |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/18692729.2023.2191478 |journal=Contemporary Japan |volume=36 |issue=2 |language=en |pages=200–221 |doi=10.1080/18692729.2023.2191478 |s2cid=257800021 |issn=1869-2729}} All {{ill|Prefectures of Japan|en|Prefectures of Japan|qid=Q50337|lt=prefectures|1=Prefectures of Japan}} are required to maintain departments of general affairs, finance, welfare, health, and labor. Departments of agriculture, fisheries, forestry, commerce, and industry are optional, depending on local needs. The Governor is responsible for all activities supported through [[:en:local taxation|local taxation]] or the national government. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan|Politics}} * [[:en:Japanese honors system|Japanese honours system]] * {{ill|Politics of Japan|en|Politics of Japan|qid=Q865455|lt=Politics of Japan|1=Politics of Japan}} * [[Administrative structure of the Imperial Japanese Government]] * {{ill|List of Japanese government and military commanders of World War II|en|List of Japanese government and military commanders of World War II|qid=Q6575177|lt=List of Japanese
